Business Insight

AI and Business Data Analysis: You Don't Need Real-Time Big Data

Financials, costs, sales, production efficiency and demand forecasts: artificial intelligence works even with Excel, Access and data updated once a week.

March 12, 2026 · Finch-AI · 8 min read

Contents

  1. The myth of real-time Big Data
  2. Excel, Access and weekly data: why they work
  3. What AI can do with your data
  4. The real challenges for an SME
  5. Tailored AI: competitive advantage or necessity?
  6. Sources and References

The myth of real-time Big Data

There's a widespread belief among entrepreneurs: artificial intelligence is for big companies, for those with integrated ERP systems, cloud data lakes and dashboards that update every second. If your data lives in Excel sheets, in an Access database updated once a week, or in monthly reports extracted from the management system, you think you're cut off from the AI revolution. Yet document intelligence solutions show that even paper-based handling can be successfully automated.

The numbers seem to confirm this perception. According to ISTAT, in 2025 only 16.4% of Italian companies with at least 10 employees use AI technologies, and the gap between large companies and SMEs has widened to 37 percentage points[1]. The Politecnico di Milano Observatory confirms it: just 7% of small and 15% of medium-sized companies have started AI projects[2].

But this narrative hides a fundamental error: it confuses the frequency of data updates with the ability to analyze it intelligently. These are two completely different things.

Excel, Access and weekly data: why they work

The key point is this: what matters is the dynamics of the system, not the speed at which you collect the data. For most manufacturing, trading or service SMEs, the fundamental business parameters — raw-material costs, sales margins, production efficiency, demand patterns — don't change every minute. They change over weekly, monthly, seasonal horizons.

If the price of steel changes every two weeks and you update your costs every week, you have more than enough data for serious predictive analysis. If your sales follow seasonal cycles with monthly variations, a monthly-updated dataset captures the real dynamics of your market perfectly.

💡 The principle of adequate frequency

In signal theory there's the Nyquist theorem: to capture a signal without losing information, you just need to sample it at twice its maximum frequency. The same principle applies to business data. If your market parameters change on a weekly basis, you need at least two updates a week to capture the real dynamics. If they change monthly, twice a month is enough. The point is that for many SMEs the rate of change of key parameters is already compatible with existing data-collection rhythms — and even where it isn't, going from one weekly update to two is a minimal adjustment compared to the value you get.

Of course, the current geopolitical situation affects energy costs with greater volatility than usual. Tensions in the Middle East risk adding up to €10 billion in energy costs to Italian companies in 2026, with increases of 13.5% on gas and electricity[3]. But even here, the variation happens over scales of days and weeks — not milliseconds. Biweekly or even weekly monitoring of energy costs, fed even just by an Excel export, lets an AI system detect trends, anomalies and margin impacts with enough lead time to act.

What AI can do with your data (even imperfect data)

Let's see concretely what becomes possible when a tailor-made artificial-intelligence system starts analyzing the data you already have, even if it's in Excel or Access.

Financial statement and numbers analysis

An AI can read your financial-statement data — even extracted manually from the management system once a month — and automatically identify anomalous variations, margin trends, imbalances between costs and revenue, and flag areas of financial risk in advance. You don't need a real-time feed from the bank account: you need a reliable periodic snapshot. This approach is similar to what we use for automation in professional firms, where the accuracy of historical data is fundamental.

Sales monitoring and price-list verification

Comparing prices charged with agreed price lists, cross-referencing sales data with actual margins, identifying customers or products where you're losing margin: these analyses require transaction data that most SMEs already have in their management systems. AI turns them from static tables into operational insights, with automatic alerts when something deviates from the norm.

Cost monitoring in real time (or close to it)

Monitoring energy and raw-material costs doesn't require million-euro IoT sensors. An Excel file with monthly bills, weekly consumption by department or supplier quotes becomes the fuel for models that forecast cost evolution and suggest corrective actions. Studies show that AI solutions for energy monitoring can cut costs by 10-25% even in small-scale manufacturing contexts[4].

Production efficiency: kWh per machine, kWh per batch

One of the most underrated areas is monitoring energy efficiency at the level of a single machine or a single production batch. Even without a sophisticated MES system, many companies already record — perhaps on an Excel sheet — consumption per shift, per production line or per job. An AI can analyze this data and answer concrete questions: which machine consumes the most per unit produced? Which batch had anomalous efficiency? Is there a correlation between the operator, the shift and energy consumption?

In a context where energy costs weigh more and more on the income statement, turning raw consumption data into production-efficiency KPIs — kWh per piece, kWh per batch, energy cost per order — becomes a measurable competitive advantage. Research published on ScienceDirect shows that AI models applied to manufacturing energy-consumption data can identify anomalies and predict deviations even starting from data collected at non-continuous intervals[5]. Integrating this data with document intelligence for logistics can optimize the entire value chain.

Demand forecasting

Demand forecasting is perhaps the area where AI offers the most evident leap in quality, even with periodic data. Traditional models based on moving averages or simple seasonality can be replaced by algorithms that cross-reference sales history, external factors (weather, events, market trends) and macroeconomic data. Companies that adopt AI for forecasting record a 30-50% reduction in forecasting errors[6], with direct impacts on inventory management and purchasing.

Analysis areaTypical SME data frequencyAI added value
Financials and P&LMonthlyAnomaly detection, trend analysis, early warning
Sales and marginsWeekly/MonthlyPrice-list checks, margin by customer/product
Energy costsWeekly/MonthlyCost forecasting, consumption optimization
kWh per machine/batchPer shift/WeeklyBenchmarking, anomalies, energy OEE
Demand forecastingMonthlyDemand prediction, stock optimization
Raw-material costsWeeklyPrice trends, alerting, scenario analysis

The real challenges for an SME

Being honest about the difficulties is fundamental. Adopting AI in a small or medium-sized company isn't an obstacle-free path, and downplaying the challenges would be a disservice.

Data quality is the first hurdle. Excel sheets with inconsistent formats, missing data, columns renamed every month, duplicates: the Politecnico di Milano Observatory identifies precisely the immaturity in data management as the main brake on AI adoption in SMEs[2]. The data doesn't need to be perfect, but minimal rules of structure and consistency are needed.

In-house skills are another significant barrier. Almost 60% of Italian companies cite the lack of adequate digital skills as the main obstacle to AI adoption[7]. It's not about hiring data scientists: it's about having at least one person who understands what AI can do and can talk with those who implement it.

Resistance to change is perhaps the subtlest challenge. The administrative manager who has analyzed the numbers "by eye" and "by experience" for twenty years may perceive AI as a threat. In reality, a good AI system doesn't replace human experience: it enhances it, freeing up time from the repetitive tasks of collecting and aggregating data to devote it to interpretation and decision-making.

Perceived cost blocks many SMEs before they even explore the options. The AI market in Italy reached €1.8 billion in 2025 with 50% growth[8], but much of this spending is concentrated in large companies. For an SME, the point isn't to invest millions: it's to find solutions proportionate to its turnover and its data.

⚠️ Regulatory uncertainty

47.3% of Italian companies cite uncertainty about the regulatory framework (the European AI Act, GDPR, sector regulations) as a brake on adoption[7]. A competent technology partner can guide the SME through compliance without it becoming a further obstacle.

Tailored AI: not just an advantage, a necessity

The challenges are real, but none is insurmountable. And the cost of not acting is becoming higher and higher.

In a market where energy costs can swing 13% in a few months for geopolitical reasons[3], where supply chains are volatile and where competitors — international ones too — adopt ever more sophisticated tools to optimize every aspect of the business, flying blind is no longer a sustainable strategy.

The key isn't buying a generic "AI" software and hoping it works. The key is having artificial intelligence built for you: one that knows your sector, understands the structure of your data (even if it's in Excel), adapts to your update rhythms and speaks the language of your business. A system that knows your production data arrives every Monday morning, that the financials close on the 15th of the month, that supplier price lists update quarterly — and that works with these constraints, not against them.

But just as important is having a partner who supports you on this journey. Not a vendor who sells you a license and disappears, but someone who understands your operational challenges, helps you structure the data progressively, configures the models to your specific needs and grows together with your company. For an SME, this partnership is often more decisive than the technology itself.

AI for SMEs isn't a matter of having perfect data or the most advanced systems. It's a matter of having the right partner to turn the data you already have into better decisions.

58% of Italian SMEs say they are interested in AI[2]. But interest without action remains a missed opportunity. Those who move now — even starting from a simple Excel file and a concrete question — build a competitive advantage that will become increasingly hard to close. Those who wait for their data to be "ready" risk waiting forever, while the market moves on without them.

📚 Sources and References

  2. "Artificial Intelligence Observatory 2025 – The AI market in Italy", Artificial Intelligence Observatory, Politecnico di Milano, February 2025. Link ↗ ↩

  4. "AI Energy Efficiency for Manufacturing – Cut Factory Costs", Signalo / Xsaverio, 2025. Link ↗ ↩
  5. "Integrating artificial intelligence into energy management", ScienceDirect – Energy and AI, 2025. Link ↗ ↩
